title: Confidence interval for slope
description: Practice making a t interval to estimate a population slope from sample data.

assessment_items.question_content:
Musa is interested in the relationship between hours spent studying and caffeine consumption among students at his school. He randomly selects $20$ students at his school and records their caffeine intake (mg) and the amount of time spent studying in a given week. Here is computer output from a least-squares regression analysis on his sample:

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
:- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $2.544$ | $0.134$ | $18.955$ | $0.000$
**Caffeine**| $0.164$| $0.057$ | $2.862$ | $0.010$
$\text{S}=1.532$| $\text{R-sq}=60.0\%$| | |

Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of these is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the slope of the least squares regression line?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a $t$ interval estimating slope:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right) 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
:- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $2.544$ | $0.134$ | $18.955$ | $0.000$
**Caffeine**| $\blueD{0.164}$| $\purpleD{0.057}$ | $2.862$ | $0.010$
$\text{S}=1.532$| $\text{R-sq}=60.0\%$| | |

Our statistic is the sample slope $b=\blueD{0.164}$.

Our standard error of the sample slope is $\text{SE}_b=\purpleD{0.057}$.

Our sample size is $n=20$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=20-2=18$, so for $95\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.101$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right)  \\\\
\blueD{0.164} &\pm 2.101(\purpleD{0.057})
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Sebastian was interested in the relationship between the driver's age and the probability to be involved in a car accident. He collected data about accidents in the year $2009$ and calculated the number of accidents per $100$ drivers for every age from $16$ to $24$ years (a total of $9$ different age levels). Here is computer output from a least-squares regression analysis on his sample:

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $39.11$ | $5.49$ | $7.12$ | $0.000$
**Age** | $-1.133$ | $0.27$ | $-4.16$ | $0.004$
$\text{S}=2.11$| $\text{R-sq}=67.1\%$| | |

Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of these is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the slope of the least squares regression line?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a $t$ interval estimating slope:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right) 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $39.11$ | $5.49$ | $7.12$ | $0.000$
**Age** | $\blueD{-1.133}$ | $\purpleD{0.27}$ | $-4.16$ | $0.004$
$\text{S}=2.11$| $\text{R-sq}=67.1\%$| | |

Our statistic is the sample slope $b=\blueD{-1.133}$.

Our standard error of the sample slope is $\text{SE}_b=\purpleD{0.27}$.

Our sample size is $n=9$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=9-2=7$, so for $95\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.365$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right)  \\\\
\blueD{-1.133} &\pm 2.365(\purpleD{0.27})
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Janine is studying the relationship between the size of a diamond (in carats) and its price. She obtains size and price data for a random sample of $64$ diamonds. Here is computer output from a least-squares regression analysis on her sample:

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $8.00$ | $60.76$ | $0.13$ | $0.90$
**Carat**| $2722.34$| $259.92$ | $10.47$ | $0.00$
$\text{S}=132.30$| $\text{R-sq}=63.9\%$| | |

Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of these is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the slope of the least squares regression line?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a $t$ interval estimating slope:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right) 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $8.00$ | $60.76$ | $0.13$ | $0.90$
**Carat**| $\blueD{2722.34}$| $\purpleD{259.92}$ | $10.47$ | $0.00$
$\text{S}=132.30$| $\text{R-sq}=63.9\%$| | |

Our statistic is the sample slope $b=\blueD{2722.34}$.

Our standard error of the sample slope is $\text{SE}_b=\purpleD{259.92}$.

Our sample size is $n=64$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=64-2=62$, so for $95\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.00$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right)  \\\\
\blueD{2722.34} &\pm 2.00(\purpleD{259.92})
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Mekhi is studying the trend of the world's average temperature over time. He collects data about the world's average temperature between the years $1970$ and $2011$ (a total of $42$ years). Here is computer output from a least-squares regression analysis on his sample (years are counted as number of years since $1970$):

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $13.964$ | $0.028$ | $506.83$ | $0.00$
**Year** | $0.0167$ | $0.001$ | $14.79$ | $0.00$
$\text{S}=0.092$| $\text{R-sq}=83.83\%$| | |

Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of these is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the slope of the least squares regression line?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a $t$ interval estimating slope:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right) 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $13.964$ | $0.028$ | $506.83$ | $0.00$
**Year** | $\blueD{0.0167}$ | $\purpleD{0.001}$ | $14.79$ | $0.00$
$\text{S}=0.092$| $\text{R-sq}=83.83\%$| | |

Our statistic is the sample slope $b=\blueD{0.0167}$.

Our standard error of the sample slope is $\text{SE}_b=\purpleD{0.001}$.

Our sample size is $n=42$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=42-2=40$, so for $95\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.021$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right)  \\\\
\blueD{0.0167} &\pm 2.021(\purpleD{0.001})
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Amos collected data about population density (in thousand people per square kilometer) and average rent for $1$-bedroom apartments in a random sample of $25$ cities. Here is computer output from a least-squares regression analysis on his sample:

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P

**Constant** | $812$ | $109.8$ | $7.393$ | $0.000$
**Density** | $22.615$ | $4.179$ | $5.411$ | $0.000$
$\text{S}=262.1$| $\text{R-sq}=54.09\%$| | |

Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of these is a $90\%$ confidence interval for the slope of the least squares regression line?** 

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Formula

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P

**Constant** | $812$ | $109.8$ | $7.393$ | $0.000$
**Density** | $\blueD{22.615}$ | $\purpleD{4.179}$ | $5.411$ | $0.000$
$\text{S}=262.1$| $\text{R-sq}=54.09\%$| | |

Our statistic is the sample slope $b=\blueD{22.615}$.

Our standard error of the sample slope is $\text{SE}_b=\purpleD{4.179}$.

Our sample size is $n=25$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=25-2=23$, so for $90\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=1.714$.

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right)  \\\\

assessment_items.question_content:
Sophie wanted to know if sons of taller fathers tend to be taller.  She collected data about the heights of a random sample of $110$ men and about their fathers' heights. Here is computer output from a least-squares regression analysis on her sample:

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $100.64$ | $10.1$ | $9.960$ | $0.000$
**Father's height** | $0.431$ | $0.059$ | $7.369$ | $0.000$
$\text{S}=5.673$| $\text{R-sq}=21.12\%$| | |

Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of these is a $90\%$ confidence interval for the slope of the least squares regression line?**

hints:

\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $100.64$ | $10.1$ | $9.960$ | $0.000$
**Father's height** | $\blueD{0.431}$ | $\purpleD{0.059}$ | $7.369$ | $0.000$
$\text{S}=5.673$| $\text{R-sq}=21.12\%$| | |

Our statistic is the sample slope $b=\blueD{0.431}$.

Our standard error of the sample slope is $\text{SE}_b=\purpleD{0.059}$.

Our sample size is $n=110$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=110-2=108$, so for $90\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=1.66$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right)  \\\\
\blueD{0.431} &\pm 1.66(\purpleD{0.059})
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A panel of judges was asked to judge the quality of a random sample of $24$ brands of potato chips. Here is computer output from a least-squares regression analysis on the relationship between the price of each brand (in dollars per pack) and its rating:

###Components of formula

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $-0.39$ | $0.57$ | $-0.687$ | $0.499$
**Price** | $\blueD{2.52}$ | $\purpleD{0.283}$ | $8.9$ | $0.000$
$\text{S}=0.7387$| $\text{R-sq}=77.3\%$| | |

Our statistic is the sample slope $b=\blueD{2.52}$.

Our standard error of the sample slope is $\text{SE}_b=\purpleD{0.283}$.

Our sample size is $n=24$.

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right)  \\\\
\blueD{2.52} &\pm 1.717(\purpleD{0.283})
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Ona took a random sample of $20$ soccer teams across Europe, and tracked the average number of goals each team scored per match, and how many total matches each team won, in the $2014\,\text{-}\,2015$ season. Here is computer output from a least-squares regression analysis on her sample:

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $-3.64 $ | $1.54$ | $-2.36$ | $0.03$
**Goals** | $14.02$ | $1.15$ | $12.17$ | $0.00$
$\text{S}=1.951$| $\text{R-sq}=88.56\%$| | |

Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of these is a $99\%$ confidence interval for the slope of the least squares regression line?** 

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a $t$ interval estimating slope:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right) 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $-3.64 $ | $1.54$ | $-2.36$ | $0.03$
**Goals** | $\blueD{14.02}$ | $\purpleD{1.15}$ | $12.17$ | $0.00$
$\text{S}=1.951$| $\text{R-sq}=88.56\%$| | |

Our statistic is the sample slope $b=\blueD{14.02}$.

Our standard error of the sample slope is $\text{SE}_b=\purpleD{1.15}$.

Our sample size is $n=20$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=20-2=18$, so for $99\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.878$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right)  \\\\
\blueD{14.02} &\pm 2.878(\purpleD{1.15})
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Elvin collected the scores of a random sample $43$ students on the first exam in a certain class and their corresponding scores on the second exam in that class. Here is computer output from a least-squares regression analysis on his sample:

**Constant** | $6.985$ | $6.65$ | $1.05$ | $0.3$    
**First exam** | $\blueD{0.881}$ | $\purpleD{0.11}$ | $8.29$ | $0.00$
$\text{S}=13.2$| $\text{R-sq}=62.89\%$| | |

Our statistic is the sample slope $b=\blueD{0.881}$.

Our standard error of the sample slope is $\text{SE}_b=\purpleD{0.11}$.

Our sample size is $n=43$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ explanation 3]]

\blueD{0.881} &\pm 2.7(\purpleD{0.11})

assessment_items.question_content:
Cars lose value the farther they are driven. Heidi collected data about the mileage (in thousands of kilometers) and value (in thousands of dollars) of a random sample of $11$ cars of the same make and model. Here is computer output from a least-squares regression analysis on her sample:

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $39.575$ | $0.765$ | $51.77$ | $0.00$
**Mileage** | $-0.246$ | $0.013$ | $-18.87$ | $0.00$
$\text{S}=1.349$| $\text{R-sq}=97.26\%$| | |

Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of these is a $99\%$ confidence interval for the slope of the least squares regression line?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a $t$ interval estimating slope:

b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right) 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Constant** | $39.575$ | $0.765$ | $51.77$ | $0.00$
**Mileage** | $\blueD{-0.246}$ | $\purpleD{0.013}$ | $-18.87$ | $0.00$
$\text{S}=1.349$| $\text{R-sq}=97.26\%$| | |

Our statistic is the sample slope $b=\blueD{-0.246}$.

Our standard error of the sample slope is $\text{SE}_b=\purpleD{0.013}$.

Our sample size is $n=11$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=11-2=9$, so for $99\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=3.25$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
b&\pm t_{n-2}^*\left(\text{SE}_b\right)  \\\\
\blueD{-0.246} &\pm 3.25(\purpleD{0.013})
\end{align}$

Christine collected data about the weight (in thousand $\text{kg}$) and fuel efficiency (in $\text{km}$ per $\text{L}$) of a random sample of $32$ car models. Here is computer output from a least-squares regression analysis on her sample:

Predictor | Coef | SE Coef | T | P
